sentences.text: Yes.
And that was naturally a very traumatic experience.
Mostly, I didn't prepare my-- well, I wasn't prepared because I never went to a funeral before.
But being the daughter, or a blood relation, in the Hungarian Jewish customs, it was that the children of the deceased, or the parents, have to cut in their clothing.
So I had a jacket on.
And they cut the [? revere ?] of the jacket in about two inches, which was an indication that a blood relation of a close-- brother, parents, child-- has passed, passed away.
I can't remember actually the funeral, standing at the grave.
I can't remember that.
But I definitely-- excuse me-- definitely remember when they cut in.
And I even asked, why is that?
And they did explain.
Of course, my grandmother, who, who just lost her daughter was devastated.
So was her sister, who had the little child, one-year-old.
And we lived on in that apartment with Grandma.
In the meantime, the, the situation in Hungary and in Budapest became worse and worse.
The men were taken away for forced labor.
So my auntie's husband was taken.
And we only were three adult women, my brother, the little daughter of my auntie, and myself.
So it was a limited members in the family.
No.
My grandfather died long ago.
He, he died, I think, in 1934.
'36, something like that.
I remember him.
I remember him pleasantly.
He was a nice old man.
And he was only 60 or 62 when he died.
But he was in-- in those days, a 62 year old was halfway to the grave.
But he had-- he was diabetic, and I think that's what took him to the-- at the end.
Yes.
Ah, not known, but I heard.
Don't forget that we had a very limited access to the public.
We weren't allowed to go out after dark.
And you know, as I said, the restrictions came always worse and worse, where you weren't allowed to go to cinemas or to go to any of the amusements.
So we only lived.
We-- we didn't even cook ourselves.
We still had our housekeeper, who was very decent to us, and so on.
But that was it.
Now, that was about the time in '43, '44, beginning of '44, when they brought out a law that [INAUDIBLE] those people who are not Hungarian citizens.
So by that time, my father was in Brussels.
And in '42, the Germans came to Brussel and took him to Auschwitz.
That was him.
Did you-- how did you find out that information?
We got the letters back what we have sent him.
Actually, my mother's heart broke over the letters, which came back, because we knew that Brussels been taken by the Germans.
And what my father only wrote in, in a flower language, if you know what I mean, that he had made himself an accident on his hands so that he could be as an invalid.
So he thought that they're not going to take him away.
To Auschwitz, because they knew that the Jews are gathered in Brussels as well, the same way as everywhere else.
And then they take them somewhere.
Of course, he didn't know what Auschwitz-- I don't think so, that he knew what Auschwitz was or anything similar.
Only after the war did I search for him.
And then I got the notification from the Belgium Jewish Welfare that he-- he was taken to Auschwitz.
And they did give me a death certificate that he was taken and gone.
So.
Not as a child.
That was-- it was after the war.
So I was about 17, 18.
Yes, I knew.
I was told that we should be very quiet.
And we have to be humble to grandma and to Auntie because they took us in.
And we haven't got a father.
We are half orphans.
And in a way, unfortunately, it was rubbed into us quite often that we don't have our own home.
We are not with our parents.
We are-- [SIGHS]
How should I explain it to you?
Not a second-rate citizen, but a second rate in the family, that there was everybody else who had closer relations to my auntie or to grandma.
We were two teenagers who did not have the affection from parents, who didn't have any parents, who leaned on each other.
I only know that many times I have protected my brother.
And I said, nobody's allowed to say anything bad about him.
I am his sister.
I can say it.
But I am not allowing anybody to say anything bad about him, whatever it was, whether it was anti [INAUDIBLE] or-- you know.
Yes, very.
I was always the leader, a protective person, to whoever was the weaker in my surrounding.
And later on in years, it came out too.
Well, I'll tell you when I had the first danger.

Because we were not Hungarian citizens, and already full orphans, because mother died and my brother, we had to go once a month to a building where we had to show that we are still in Hungary, that we did not escape, that the two of us are going to be called later on in time-- there was no time given, and we were hoping it never comes to it-- to go to a center.
They're going to take us who are not citizens of Hungary, to somewhere where, as we later found out-- and we had somebody else who went there and was shot into the Russian river, Dnieper.
I don't whether you ever heard-- anyway.
Not Russian, Polish.
I can't even remember where it is.
What they've done to these people, that they gathered them together by a few hundred.
And then a train was taken, like trains to the concentration camps.
[SIGHS] And they were taken in those trains.
And as far as we heard after, they had the same sort of-- finish off their life that they put them on the edge of the river and shot them in.
They found their bodies after somehow.
And we were the first group, Freddie and I, who had to go to this.
So eventually, they said, anyone who has no Hungarian citizenship has to go here and there.
And our district was in that particular area.
We arrived there with our little suitcases, toothbrush, pajama, one-day clothing change.
And they kept us there.
And they kept us there for weeks on end.
Yes.
In Budapest, very close, where in one of the church-- synagogue's backyard, in a-- in a complex where there was a garden raised-- not even garden part, but a, a-- how shall I say what it is?
It's concreted.
So it was-- a yard.
But it was no flowers or no greenery, like a school yard, right?
Where kids march up and down, or whatever they do.
So in that-- and there were rooms around that yard where we were sleeping.
Now, there happened something.
I was, as I said, 16 going onto 17.
And my brother was a year younger.
And every day there were, I think, weekly, changing overseers.
They're not necessarily Nazis.
They weren't Germans.
They were Hungarians.
And they were employed by the government to oversee these few hundred or whatever Jews were there.
We were the youngest, Freddie and I.
No, no.
There were other-- people who didn't-- who were not citizens of Hungary.
Nowhere.
We were Nansen.
That was called Nansen.
We were stateless.
They could do with us whatever they wanted because we did not belong to Hungary or to Austria, where my father came from.
And we didn't belong to any other country.
We lived in Hungary.
We were born in Hungary.
But we weren't citizens of Hungary.
Right.
So there was, as I said from government, employed detectives, as we called them, who were watching the main gate so that we-- who were in that school yard, do not walk out.
And he was a man.
I was-- can't emphasize.
I was 16 going onto 17.
And he must have been around about 30.
I can't recall him exactly.
And well, he said to me, if I come to his apartment home, he'll take me out.
And he will rescue my brother and myself.
But what is that English expression?
In return of favors?
Mhm.
In return of sexual favors?
Favors, that's right.
Anyway, so I did go, and he did let us go.
So he kept his word.
And would you believe, 20 years after I saw him in Australia in Sydney.
But that's besides the point.
So anyway, he, he was very decent.
He took me to a cafe house and gave me cakes.
And I was very intimidated.
I was so shy and so closed within myself.
I couldn't even talk.
So it was really just, from as far as he's concerned, a sexual contact.
I, I was a young girl, but a very good dancing body.
Because by the way, that I forgot to tell you.
I wanted to become a ballerina.
And my father had some connections with stage people.
And when he left, he made me a promise that I can go to a certain ballet school as long as I want to.
So that promise I got.
And I did go.
So from the minute he left Budapest, I went to the ballet school not far from us until the cutoff was that I couldn't go out the street, et cetera, et cetera.
And after the war, I, I continued.
Mhm.
But I had always a very dancing good body, ballet, classical ballet.
And he took a fancy.
And as I said, that went on for a few weeks.
And he took after-- when he knew that there's danger coming, because he knew, he took us out, my Freddie and myself, and went to Grandma.
Mhm.
That's right.
He stayed in the holding.
He took me out at two o'clock in the afternoon, brought me back at 6:00 in the afternoon, right?
And whenever-- well, not whenever, once, when he knew that there was danger, that they're taking the whole group and taking them to the train station, he took Freddie and I, and let us go.
Yes.
But by that time, we didn't believe in the same apartment.
But it was more the Jewish star, the-- in-- not ghetto.
But it was a house where we gathered together and were-- it was a Jewish star because there was this, this Star of David outside the house.
And there were all Jews in one apartment, about three or four families, depends how many there were.
And we came back to this apartment.
Yes.
They were members of my auntie's husband, his parents, and his two sisters, and their children.
Men were nonexistent by the time.
They were all in the forced labor.
Only grandpa, the old man.
So the-- my uncle's parents.
And he was, by that time, early 70s.
So he- and he spoke Russian because he was a-- First World War--
Not captain.
He was captured by the Russians at the time.
And he spoke-- he learned in the-- in the camp Russian.
And so when we eventually had the Russians coming in, he saved us quite often because the Russians were coming into the house, and et cetera.
But that is later on.
So anyway, we came back with my brother to Grandma and to Auntie.
Mhm.
[SIGHS] We didn't have beds.
We had mattresses on the floor.
And I can't recall really did I-- was I ever friendly to anybody in that holding center?
It-- I can't remember how long we were there.
Was it two weeks, or four weeks, or six weeks?
I can't recall that.
I only know that this detective took me out quite often.
He fed me with goodies, which I had no way of tasting it earlier.
And the living conditions, we, we got food.
I don't know.
Oh, I remember something.
There were-- there were doves flying around.
And there was a little house for the doves.
And I remember I went up to that dove house, and took a dove.
And we killed it.
And we ate it-- cooked it and ate it.
And then-- yeah.
And I-- that I remember, something with the dove, something happened.
I, I can't recall anymore.
I know I climbed up.
I know that I took a dove.
I know that we killed it.
Who did the killing, I don't know.
But I remember eating it.
Yes.
Yes.
We were hungry.
We did get food from the Jewish Welfare, but it was never enough for all these people who were there.
And of course, every day there were more.
They brought in more into that center, into that gathering center.
Well, that was between March and-- yes-- between March and September.
Actually, that was early March.
It was in March, April.
That's right.

Because I remember that the Germans had brought out a law where they said that all people between 16 and 60, all women, should go to a sports ground, if you can imagine, a sports ground here at Moore Park.
And we gathered there in the morning at eight o'clock with a heavy sack and your absolute essential clothing.
I was not let go.
But my auntie, who had to be, who was included, had-- they let her go because she had a baby, or a one, a half, or two year old child.
That was already in 1944.
No.
Yes.
We went first to the synagogue holding center.
And to be quite honest, I don't know was that in '44 or, or in '43.
Yeah.
It could be.
It was summer.
So it must have been in July somewhere.
So I have a hunch that it must have been in '43 instead of '44.
Yes.
Yes.
We were not-- don't forget the Hungarian Christians were not gentlemans.
These Nazis, which we called [?
Nilash, ?] were out for every Hungarian Jew to go, to disappear from the earth of Hungary.
They didn't want any Jew to stay there.
As a matter of fact, which is known to history, that the Germans, when they started to take the Hungarian Jews, the Germans said, look, we haven't got any money anymore to transfer them.
Nilash ?] meant Nazi.
It's the equivalent of the German Nazis.
Yes, members of extended family.
Yes.
And we did.
And I was taken to a forced labor.
And my auntie was left-- let go.
They let her go because she had a very small child, and she had to look after the child.
My brother, that I don't know where he was at the time.
He was not with us.
I think he was in a-- I don't know.
I really don't know where he was.
I only know that at one stage, he was in a-- in Budapest, but in the mountains-- in a sanatorium, where I think he had some problem with his lungs.
And that was in the height in the mountains.
And there was, as I said, the sanatorium.
And I think that religious great uncle of the families have paid for him to stay there in that, and with false papers, or whatever it was.
I can't recall that.
I know he wasn't with us.
There were only women, and the only male was that old father of my uncle who--
Who spoke Russian.
Now, when I got into forced labor camp, that was in '44.
That's right.
And I was 15.
Was I 15 or 17?
I was born in '27.
But a very shy-- a very timid and inexperienced 17, who didn't know anything about life.
In a way, emotionally I was an adult, because I had to always lean on my own, myself.
And we, my brother and I, were protecting each other sort of.
But there was no experiences in my life.
Or, I didn't hear any, any teaching of worldly happenings.
It was somehow always-- because we didn't have the parents, and my uncle wasn't there anymore.
So most, probably the women, my auntie and my grandma, did not discuss certain things in front of us.
I only know that when I went into this forced labor camp myself, we were digging grooves.
How do you call that in English?
Sort of like trenches?
Trenches.
We were preparing.
They knew already by then that Russians are coming from one part of Hungary--
Not far from Budapest.
You know, not far.
Can't recall where.
I know we were taken, and I can't remember.
Was it a train?
Was it a bus?
Was it walking?
Again, a blank.
I remember standing at the trenches.
And coincidentally, one of my colleagues at work-- because by that time, I was 17.
And 14, I had to leave school because we weren't allowed to go to school anymore.
And I worked in a factory where they made under-- corset undergarments, elastic stockings, and elastic corsets, and brassieres, and things like that.
And there was another Jewish woman whom-- of course, that factory was full with Jews because the owner was a Jew.
[NON-ENGLISH] [LAUGHS] Again, [POINTS] just staying in there.
And she was there with me in the same forced labor camp, who was an adult of 30 years of age.
And I just somehow was hanging onto her so that she could sort of protect me, and that we hang on together, and so on and so forth.
But that forced labor didn't last long, I think four to six weeks.
And then we were left-- let back to Budapest.
Why?
I can't remember.
Did I prove something?
Or did they-- did the family in Budapest try to pull some strings?
I can't remember a thing.
I only know that I got back.
OK.
I'll just ask you a few things about the labor camp.
What were-- what were the living, living conditions like?
Don't remember.
Blank.
It somehow-- I'm trying to think.
Certain things in my life are total blanks, like somehow I blocked them out.
And I don't know under hypnosis would I remember or not.
It's irrelevant.
And I don't need to know what kind of accommodation I had there in the forced labor camp.
I can't recall it at all.
Yes.
We were digging.
That's all what we've done.
No.
No.
That I don't remember.
I didn't see any deaths then.
We were all working.
We were all between 16 and 60.
And I remember, I see that in front of me, that, that, Hungarian woman.
Aggie was standing next to me.
And we both were putting our foot on the shovel to dig, and dig, and dig, and dig.
And I can't-- there is something subconsciously in my mind that we were laughing occasionally.
I can't remember.
I remember how she looked.
But I don't remember what we had on, the clothing, and where we were.
I only remember that we had the trenches, and that's where we were digging, and full of people left and right, and opposite, and all that.
By [?
Nilash, ?] yes.
Yes.
Maybe this is why I was very shy.
I was frightened what's going to happen.
I missed the family.
I missed my brother.
I missed my grandmother, my auntie.
And there I was among strangers.
Aggi ?] was the only one whom I knew.
And we were thousands of people.
But divided not only on that one spot, we had trenches to be built in several places.
But I was in one particular.
And they were full with people whom I never met before.
I was very intimidated.
What's going to happen.
Yes.
I don't know how.
I came back to Budapest.
And then there-- of course, we all had to wear that yellow star.
Oh, that came in maybe in '42, 1942.
As a matter of fact, I had a very unpleasant incident there.
I knew already by then that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother-- that's right.
It must have been in '92, '93.
My mother was dead already.
And I went to work into this elastic factory, which was not very far from where we lived in the apartment of Grandma and Auntie.
And as I was walking-- let's say it was about four or five blocks of-- street blocks.
And as I got to a particular part where a big warehouse was, in front of the warehouse a sort of a Davy Jones or [?
Grace Brothers ?].
There was a woman standing with a basket of flowers.
And she was selling them.
But she was selling them without a permit.
And that was obvious because she was constantly looking.
Is a policeman coming?
Is a-- is a Nazi coming, or someone?
And I noticed that about 15 to 20 meters away there is uniform coming.
So I quickly went to the women.
I said, disappear because there's somebody in uniform.
Well, then she picked up the basket and shot through.
But the officer noticed it that I went to her.
And he got me by the arm.
And he said he's going to take me into the police station.
And would you believe that was in winter?
I had high boots on and some sort of a winter coat.
And I wet myself.
And it was running down my legs into the boot in fright.
